question: Define a base class `Animal` with a private `species` attribute and a parameterized constructor that initializes it. Create a derived class `Dog` with an additional private `name` attribute. The `Dog` constructor must correctly call the `Animal` base class constructor to initialize the `species` and then initialize its own `name`. Include a `makeSound()` method in both classes.

test_cases.expected:
Animal makes a generic sound. Species: Lion
Woof! My name is Buddy. (Species initialized in base: Golden Retriever)
Animal makes a generic sound. Species: Labrador

errors: `Animal::makeSound` is not declared `virtual`. When a `Dog` object is accessed through an `Animal` pointer (polymorphic call), `Animal::makeSound` is invoked instead of `Dog::makeSound`, which is a robustness issue in object-oriented design when polymorphic behavior is expected.
